content:
First note that if we clear denominators by multiplying the given identity by \(2013x\), we get the polynomial identity

\[
2013x\bigl(f(x-!1)+f(x)+f(x+1)\bigr)\;=\;f(x)^2,
\]
which must hold as an identity in \(x\).  If \(\deg f=d\), the left‐hand side has degree \(d+1\) while the right‐hand side has degree \(2d\).  Equating degrees forces
\[
d+1 \;=\;2d
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad d=1,
\]
so \(f\) is linear.  Write
\[
f(x)=Ax+B.
\]
Then
\[
f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)
=\bigl(A(x-1)+B\bigr)+(Ax+B)+\bigl(A(x+1)+B\bigr)
=3(Ax+B),
\]
and
\[
\frac{f(x)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{(Ax+B)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{A^2x^2+2ABx+B^2}{2013x}
=\frac{A^2}{2013}\,x\;+\;\frac{2AB}{2013}\;+\;\frac{B^2}{2013x}.
\]
Matching to \(3(Ax+B)\) forces the \(x^{-1}\)–term to vanish, so \(B^2=0\) and hence \(B=0\).  Then
\[
3A x \;=\;\frac{A^2}{2013}\,x
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
3A=\frac{A^2}{2013}.
\]
Since \(A\neq0\) (nonconstant \(f\)), we get \(A=3\cdot2013=6039\).  Thus
\[
f(x)=6039x,
\]
and in particular
\[
f(1)=6039.
\]
There are no other solutions, so the sum of all possible values of \(f(1)\) is
\[
\boxed{6039}.
\]

2013x\bigl(f(x-!1)+f(x)+f(x+1)\bigr)\;=\;f(x)^2,

d+1 \;=\;2d

\]

\[
f(x)=Ax+B.
\]

\[
f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)
=\bigl(A(x-1)+B\bigr)+(Ax+B)+\bigl(A(x+1)+B\bigr)
=3(Ax+B),
\]

\[
\frac{f(x)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{(Ax+B)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{A^2x^2+2ABx+B^2}{2013x}

\]
Matching to \(3(Ax+B)\) forces the \(x^{-1}\)–term to vanish, so \(B^2=0\) and hence \(B=0\).  Then
\[
3A x \;=\;\frac{A^2}{2013}\,x

3A=\frac{A^2}{2013}.

Since \(A\neq0\) (nonconstant \(f\)), we get \(A=3\cdot2013=6039\).  Thus

\[
